Civic Education

vi
Preface
The Civic Education Teaching Module for Form 1 has been developed following the 
introduction of the Competence Based Curriculum (CBC) which marks a transformative 
step in improving education quality and relevance. This Teaching Module has been designed 
to support teachers navigate as they effectively deliver Civic Education lessons under the new 
curriculum. The module aims to bridge the gap created by the absence of approved textbooks 
and to equip educators with practical tools, activities, and assessments tailored to the learners’ 
level and needs.
As a Ministry, we understand the challenges that come with transitioning to a new curriculum 
framework. This module reflects our commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible 
for teachers, by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and formative assessments 
that align with the CBC’s objectives. However, teachers are encouraged to supplement this 
module with their own research and innovations to address any gaps that may arise during 
implementation.
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical 
and critical thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different 
school contexts. Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom 
dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches when necessary. 
We hope this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate the new 
competence-based curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners with essential 
competences, knowledge, skills and attitudes that prepare them to live responsible lives and be 
protected from life threatening vices
Kelvin Mambwe (Dr.)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services

How to use this Teaching Module
To effectively use this Civic Education Teaching Module for Form 1:
 
 Read and familiarise yourself fully with the module’s content, learning activities, and 
assessment to understand what learn will learn.
 
 Use the module’s suggested teaching and learning activities to come up a lesson plan 
specifically meant for your learners 
 
 Use a variety of teaching methods, including demonstrations, discussions, group 
work to Connect the module’s content to real-life scenarios and current events
 
 Encourage active learning by asking open-ended questions, promoting critical 
thinking, analytical thinking and fostering problem-solving skills.
 
 Assess learners learning regularly, using the module’s suggested assessment strategies 
and tools.
 
 Provide feedback and support to learners, helping them to identify areas for 
improvement and develop their skills.
 
 Monitor learner progress and adjust your teaching strategies as needed, to ensure that 
all learners meet the learning objectives
By following these steps, you can effectively use this Civics Education Teaching Module for 
Form 1 to support your teaching and promote learning

Civic Education
Teaching Module
x
Introduction
The Civic Education Teaching Module covers topics for term 2. The Teaching Module has 
been developed in line with Competence Based Curriculum (CBC) in order to support teachers 
navigate as they effectively deliver Civic Education lessons under the new curriculum. The main 
aim of the module to bridge the gap created by the absence of approved teaching and learning 
instructional materials responsive to the Competence Based Curriculum. Further it is envisaged 
that the module will equip necessary information, practical tools, activities, and assessments 
tailored to the learners’ level and needs. It must be emphasised that the module is not a traditional 
course book or lesson plan, instead, it is designed to be used in conjunction with other resources 
and materials, and provides a framework for teachers to design and deliver instruction that is 
tailored to the needs, and abilities of their learners. and learning environments.
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical 
and twenty first Century skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different 
school contexts. Teachers are therefore encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique 
classroom dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches when and where necessary. 
It is hoped that this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate the 
new competence-based curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners with 
essential competences, knowledge, skills, values and attitude are aimed at enabling learners to 
live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities and the Zambian society. 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials
Teaching and learning materials play a crucial role in facilitating effective learning. For Civic 
Education, these materials are particularly important because they help learners develop critical 
thinking, problem solving, and citizenship skills. The following are some of the reasons why 
teaching and learning materials are significant:
1.	 Enhance understanding: Teaching and learning materials help learners grasp complex 
civic concepts.
2.	 Promote engagement: Interactive materials encourage participation and discussion.
3.	 Develop critical thinking: Materials like case studies and scenarios foster critical 
thinking and problem-solving
The following are some examples of teaching and learning materials for Civic Education: 
textbooks, case studies, videos, role-playing activities, newspapers/articles, civic education 
websites, Guest speakers and debate topics 
Learning Environment Set Up
The learning environment set up can provide a comprehensive framework for designing learning 
experiences and assessments that are relevant, practical, and effective in developing learners’ 
competencies
1.	 Natural Environment: Real-world settings, such as workplaces, communities, or 
everyday life situations.
2.	 Artificial Environment: Simulated or controlled settings, such as classrooms, training 
rooms, or simulations.
3.	 Technological Environment: Digital platforms, tools, and resources, such as online 
learning platforms, simulations, or virtual reality.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
xi
Suggested Teaching Methodology
In a competence-based curriculum, the following methodologies can be effective:
1.	 Project-based learning: Encourages learners to apply skills and knowledge to real-
world projects.
2.	 Problem-based learning: Develops critical thinking and problem-solving skills 
through real-world scenarios.
3.	 Discussion-based learning: Encourage learners to discuss civic issues, promoting 
critical thinking and problem-solving.
4.	 Guest speakers and field trips: Invite experts and organize field trips to provide 
students with hands-on experience and exposure to civic institutions.
5.	 Case studies: Analyses real-life situations, promoting critical thinking and decision-
making.
6.	 Role-playing: Develops practical skills, such as communication and teamwork. For 
example, se role-playing activities to demonstrate civic concepts, such as voting or 
community engagement
7.	 Simulations: Mimics real-world situations, allowing learners to practice and apply 
skills in a controlled environment.  For example, Organize simulations of civic 
processes, such as mock elections or town hall meetings.
8.	 Inquiry-based learning: Encourages learners to explore and investigate topics, 
developing critical thinking and research skill
9.	 Media analysis: Analyse media coverage of civic issues, teaching learners to critically 
evaluate information
10.	Service learning: Integrates community service with academic learning, promoting 
civic engagement and skill development
11.	Group work: Assign group projects that promote teamwork, collaboration, and 
community engagement
Time Allocation
The standard period allocation for Civic Education for Ordinary Level Secondary School has 
been prescribed in the Zambia Education Curriculum Framework (ZECF) of 2023. The minimum 
learner-teacher contact time for the Ordinary Level Secondary School is 3 hours and 20 minutes 
per week, translating into five (5) periods for the learning area. The duration of a single period 
at this level is 40 minutes
Assessment
In a Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), assessments should focus on evaluating learners’ 
ability to apply civic knowledge, skills, and values in real-world contexts. Some suitable 
assessment types include:
1.	 Performance tasks: Learners complete tasks that mimic real-world civic scenarios.
2.	 Project-based assessments: Learners work on projects that demonstrate civic 
competencies.
3.	 Portfolio assessments: Learners collect and reflect on their civic learning experiences.
4.	 Debates and discussions: Learners participate in debates and discussions on civic 
issues.

Civic Education
Teaching Module
xii
5.	 Service learning projects: Learners to engage in community service projects that 
demonstrate civic engagement.
6.	 Case study analyses Learners analyse real-world civic cases and propose solutions.
7.	 Reflective journals: Learners reflect on their civic learning experiences and value
The Role of a Teacher
In a Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) for Civic Education, the teacher’s role is multifaceted. 
By adopting the following roles, teachers can help learners develop the competencies needed to 
become informed, engaged, and responsible citizens:
1.	 Facilitator: Guides learners in exploring civic concepts and issues. 
2.	 Mentor: Supports learners in developing critical thinking, problem-solving, and decision-
making skills.
3.	 Coach: Encourages learners to take ownership of their learning and civic engagement.
4.	 Assessor: Evaluates learners’ competencies and provides feedback.
5.	 Resource person: Provides relevant resources and information to support learner learning
6.	 To create a learning environment that promotes: critical thinking and analysis, active 
participation and engagement, collaboration and teamwork, reflection and self-assessment 
and application of civic skills and knowledge in real-life contexts

1.5 POLITICAL PARTIES
INTRODUCTION
This topic aims to introduce learners to the concept of political parties. It provides an 
understanding of the structure of a political party and how it is organised. The topic has two 
subtopics; Political Party and Political Party Systems. This topic is important in the sense that 
it promotes understanding of democracy, democratic processes and provides insight on the 
operations of political parties. Further, the topic exposes learners to the importance of tolerance- 
enabling them to appreciate divergent views in life, empathy and analytical skills to understand 
different political beliefs, thereby preparing them for future leadership roles.
General Competences
Critical Thinking
Analyse the structure and organisation of a political party
HOOK
What would happen in a democratic nation without political parties?
Key Terms
Political Party: A group of people who come together with a view of governing a 
nation
Political Power: the ability to influence or direct the actions, beliefs or conduct of 
individuals or groups.
Ideology: A set of beliefs, values and principles that shape understanding of the world 
and provide guidance to actions. Ideology can be political, social, economic or cultural 
in nature. 
Accountability: Leaders being answerable to citizens for their actions.
Transparency: The openness of leaders in their decisions and actions.
Party Manifesto: A document that contains the values and plans of a political party 
towards the nation.
One- Party System- a political system in which only one party is allowed to exist and 
hold power
Bi- Party System- a political system in which two major parties dominate the political 
landscape. It can also be called a two-party system
Multi- Party System- signifies the existence of many political parties, big and small in 
the country. They alternate power, to more than two political parties.

Civic Education
Teaching Module
2
1.5.1 POLITICAL PARTY
Introduction 
This subtopic provides insights in understanding political parties and how they operate. It 
further outlines how leaders are chosen and how political parties interact with citizens in society. 
Through this sub topic learner will learn how political parties provide a platform for citizens to 
participate in decision-making process. 
Specific Competence 
Learners to: Analyse the Organisation and Structure of a Political Party
Learning Activities
Describing a political party
Learning environment set up: 
This activity can be held from a classroom set-up.
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Pictures or videos showing activities of a party
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
Role-play
Activity Process
Divide the class into groups, and assign the following tasks for simulation
	
 each group design and form a mock political party
	
 each group to come up with a party manifesto and include key issues such as a 
party logo, ideology and campaign materials
	
 groups hold a mock meeting to discuss their key issues and ideology and party 
leaders explain their importance
	
 groups present their key issues and ideology
	
 hold a plenary session with learners to give explanations on the importance of 
having clarity of a political party’s intentions
Content Tips
A political party
A political party is a group of citizens who organize themselves with a common value, ideas and 
a goal towards forming government. A political party represents an alternative government in a 
country. Political parties originate from different aspects of our communities.
The following are some of the factors that lead to the formation of political parties:
	
 Ideological differences: disagreements over ideology, principles and policies can 
lead to the formation of new parties.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
3
	
 Economic interests: disagreements over economic policies and interests can lead to 
party formation
	
 Charismatic leaders: individuals with persuasive and compelling attitudes can lead 
others in the formation of a political party
	
 Human nature (inborn differences): by nature, people differ in the way they 
understand and look at issues of life. Therefore, differences in opinion lead people to 
form political parties
	
 Religious and communal sentiments: some people form political parties according 
to their religious beliefs
	
 Regional and geographic interests: parties may form to represent the interests of 
specific regions or geographic areas. For example, in the United States of America, 
the Republican Party initially represented the interests of the Northern states, while 
the Democratic Party, the Southern states
	
 Social and cultural issues: parties may form around social and cultural issues such 
as slavery, civil rights or immigration
	
 Historical events and crises: major historical events such as wars, economic 
depressions or social unrest may lead to the formation of parties. 
Learning Activities
Differentiating between ruling party and an opposition party
Learning Environment Set Up: 
This activity can be conducted from a classroom environment 
Teaching and Learning Materials: Charts showing responses from learners, depicting 
the ruling and opposition parties’ roles and responsibilities
Suggetsed Teaching Methods: 
Group work
Activity Process
Divide the learners into groups and assign them activities to identify the different roles 
played by the ruling and opposition Political Parties based on the Zambian scenario.
Each group to make a presentation based on the discussion.
Content Tips
Differences between the ruling and opposition parties
a.	 Ruling party

Civic Education
Teaching Module
4
	
 The ruling party is the political party which governs a country after winning 
elections and holds the governing power.
	
 It forms the executive arm of government (cabinet) headed by the president which 
is responsible for policy formulation and implementation.
	
 In the legislature, the ruling party initiates laws through public bills in line with 
its vision and development goals as enshrined in the party manifesto.
	
 It mobilises citizens to support national initiatives and government programmes.
b.	 Opposition party
	
 An opposition political party is any political party which is not in government 
but works to provide checks and balances on the ruling party. This is done by 
questioning the actions, policies and decisions of government in power. 
	
 It ensures that the government remains answerable to the citizens by holding 
government accountable for its actions
	
 It provides a healthy democracy by ensuring that government is accountable, 
transparent and responsive to the needs of the citizens.
	
 It presents alternative policies and programmes for development purposes and 
stands as an alternative government.
	
 It educates the public on important national and international issues
	
 Opposition political parties raise public debates that in turn force government 
consider popular concerns in the interest of the public
Learning Activities
Outlining the structure of a political party (National Executive Committee, Provincial 
Executive Committee, District Executive Committee, Constituency Committee, 
Sections and Branches)
Learning environment Set Up: 
This activity can be conducted from a classroom set up
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Charts on which learners draw their diagramming responses to visualize the structure of 
political parties, depicting the centralised and the decentralised types of party structures
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
Group work
Activity Process
Organise the class as a political party with structures at National, Provincial, District, 
Constituency, Ward and Section levels.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
5
Let each structure apportion positions to its members 
Content Tips
Political Party Structure
Every political party is structured as follows:
a.	 The National Executive Committee (NEC): this is the highest organ of the party which 
is made up of senior elected party members
b.	 The Provincial Committee (PEC): this is a political party organ which administers par­
ty affairs at the provincial level headed by elected chairperson
c.	 The District Executive Committee (DEC): this is a political party organ established in 
every district headed by the district chairperson.
d.	 The Constituency Committee: this is a party organ which is answerable to the district 
executive and is responsible for managing the affairs of a political party at constituency 
level.
e.	 Ward and Sections or Branch Committee: These are lower organs within the party 
structure responsible for mobilizing grassroot membership for a political party. 
      
Key Positions in Political parties
At every level within the political party structure are individuals holding various key positions 
which include:
Party President: This is a leader of a political party who becomes a party candidate in the 
election.
Party Chairperson: This is a person responsible for managing the general affairs of a political 
party.
Secretary general: This is the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the party responsible for the 
day-to-day operations of the political party 
Party Treasurer: This is the official who manages the finances of a political party
Committee Chairpersons: A political party contains various committees responsible for various 
operations and levels of the political party. Each of these committees is headed by a 
chairperson.
Learning Activities
Describing the functions of a political party (Educate citizens, provide alternative 
leadership, providing checks and balances …)
Learning Environment Set Up: 
This activity can be conducted from a classroom environment 
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Chart showing functions of a political party

Civic Education
Teaching Module
6
Suggested Teaching and Learning Method: 
Debate, Guest speaker and field trip
Activity Process
1.	 Guest Speaker 
 
 Invite a guest speaker (an expert) to give a talk on the functions of a political party 
or visit a nearby political party office.
 
 Learners listen, take down some important points and ask questions based on the 
talk
2.	 Debate 
 
 Organise a debate on the importance of political parties.
 
 Hold a plenary session, discussing the importance of political parties 
 
 Learners to diagrammatically present the functions of political parties on flowcharts, 
then to the class
Content Tips
Functions of a political party
 
 Selection or nomination of political personal
 
 Formation of public policies
 
 Conducting and criticism of government
 
 Political education.
 
 International between people and government 
 
 Development and maintenance of s sense of national unity
 
 Coordinating the branches of government 
 
 Making it possible for the electorate to function in an organized and effective manner.
 
 Framing of political issue for the people
 
 Selection of candidates during elections
 
 Serving as agencies of civic education
 
 Parties develop a sense of national unity
 
 Formulate party policies
 
 They permit the electorate to function
 
 They seek to control government
 
 They minimize the crushes of economic interests for the demand of various economic 
groups find expressions in party platforms.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
7
Learning Activities
Dramatising a political campaign activity (meeting)
Learning Environment setup: 
This activity can be held from a classroom set up.
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Pictographic representation of a political campaign and materials to hold mock elections
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
role play or simulation
Activity Process
Dramatisation 
1.	 Teacher to divide class into a few groups (as political parties) to dramatize an elec­
tions campaign to elect a class monitor or members of a student council.
	
 Each group should represent a political party with pseudonyms of party names
	
 Each group to choose a learner to act as a candidate for elections
	
 Teacher to guide learners to act as party candidates in the preparation for their 
campaign messages in order to win voters over
	
 Party candidates present their campaign messages to the class (the audience)
	
 The audience listens and ask them questions
	
 Teacher and learners to summarise the importance of political campaigns
Content Tips
	
 A political party campaign is a selling of political ideas to the public in order to 
win the support of the public.
	
 it involves the dissemination of political party message to the public through 
rallies, debates, door to door canvasing, social media engangements and town 
hall meetings
	
 every political party has rights to disseminate information about its policies and 
idiologies as a means towards winning people’s trust to gain the votes
Expected Standard
Structure and organisation of a political party analysed accordingly
Assessment
1.	 Essay 
Imagine you have been elected as a leader of your party. Explore the strategies you 
would use to organise the party in order to win elections and form government.

2.	 Project 
Conduct a research on the importance of women and youth participation in political

1.5.2	 POLITICAL PARTY SYSTEMS	
INTRODUCTION
This sub-topic aims to introduce learners to the concept of political party systems. It provides an 
understanding of how each political party system is organised and operates. The importance of 
studying political party systems centres on how their operations influence governance.
Specific Competence 
Learners to: Evaluate political party systems
Learning Activities
Creating a learner platform for campaigning  
Learning Environment Setup: 
This activity can be done from either outside or inside the classroom set up
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
a graphical presentation of the characteristics and functions of systems of political 
parties.
Suggested Teaching Method: Simulation 
Activity Process
Organise an assembly for learners and ask those who want to be representatives to 
campaign in order to be elected into leadership roles such as class monitor, prefect, 
board memmber ... 
Content Tips
Learners should be given an opportunity to participate in the governance of a school. 
As such the best way to choose representatives for learners into the school council or 
school board is to allow learners elect their own represenatives. There is need for a 
workable platform for learners to campaign and be voted in. key features of a campaign 
platform inlude
 
 Campaign manifesto (name, position, promises and slogans)
 
 Campaign posters
 
 Public speaking opportunity

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
Learning Activities
Identifying party systems
Learning Environment Set Up: 
this activity can be done from a classroom set up
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
a graphical presentation of the characteristics and functions of systems of political 
parties.
Suggested Teaching Method: 
Group work
 Activity Process
Class Discussion 
Arrange the class into three different group and give them country names to discuss 
the party systems 
Country A: One party system 
Country B: Two party system
Country C: Multi-party system 
Each group to present their points based on the discussion 
Content Tips
Kinds of Party Systems
The most basic way of classifying different party systems is One-Party System, Bi or 
two- Party System and Multi-Party System:
One-Party System: This is where a country only allows the existence of one political 
party. 
Bi or Two-Party System: This is a situation where there are two major political parties 
in a country such as the Republican party and the Democrats in the United States of 
America or the Conservative Party and Labour Party in the United Kingdom. The two 
parties compete for political power 
Multi-Party System: this is where a country allows the formation of more political 
parties to compete for political power. A Good example is Zambia where there are more 
than 40 political parties.

Civic Education
Teaching Module
10
Learning Activities
Comparing and contrasting political party systems (advantages and disadvantages)
Learning Environment Setup: 
this activity can be done from a classroom set up
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Charts showing advantages and disadvantages of different political party systems 
Suggested Teaching Method: 
Debate
 Activity Process
Class Debate 
	
z Arrange the learners into two groups and ask them to debate on the advantages and 
disadvantages of One-Party and Multi-Party systems. 
	
z After the debate hold the plenary session in which the entire class can bring out the 
advantages and disadvantages of the different party systems. 
Content Tips
The One-Party, Bi- Party and the Multi-Party systems have both advantages and 
disadvantages. 
One-Party System
Advantages 
	
 One-party system brings about stable government as there is no power struggle among 
parties
	
 There is faster decision-making since there are no lengthy debates
	
 Implementing uniform policies becomes easier as there is no opposition to challenge 
or delay them
	
 One-party system can act as unifying factor, promoting national integration since there 
is no feelings to religious or ethnic differences 
	
 A single party can easily mobilize resources quickly
Disadvantages of One-Party System
	
 There is lack of transparency and accountability since there is no opposition party to 
hold the ruling party answerable thereby promoting corruption.
	
 There is suppression of individual freedoms as one-party often suffocates individual 
voices and limits choices
	
 There is a risky of dictatorship as power is concentrated in one party
	
 One party may not represent the diverse interests and aspirations of people
	
 Without opposition political parties, there will be limited political debates leading to a

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
11
lack of diverse views
	
 The lack of political competition can hinder social progress, as the ruling party may not 
be motivated to innovate or improve societal conditions
Bi-Party or Two-Party System
Advantages
	
 It ensures successful working of the parliamentary form of government in which the 
opposition members of parliament stand to provide checks and balances to the ruling 
party.
	
 There is stability of government as the ruling party typically holds the majority seats 
in parliament.
	
 The presence of the opposition party has natural effect on the efficiency of the ruling 
administration
	
 The opposition party provides an alternative for the citizens to change government
	
 Each major party plays a positive and constructive role in a bid to win the sympathy 
and gain political power 
Disadvantages
	
 It limits the choice of the electorates to only two options.
	
 It can easily promote regionalism, racism or tribalism based on two political ideologies 
thereby undermining national unity.
	
 It promotes blind devotion to a political party as individuals are stuck between two 
political ideologies.
Multi-Party System
Advantages 
	
 It gives a variety of choices to voters
	
 It gives adequate representation to numerous interests of people
	
 It promotes respect for people’s choice of political belonging.
	
 It promotes political rights by enabling people the freedom to form political parties.
	
 Acts as a powerful check on the trend of dictatorial governance.
Disadvantages
	
 Large number of political parties creates a lot of confusion by encouraging small groups 
to enter into arena of struggle for power
	
 Too many political parties might hold different political ideologies some of which 
might be inimical to the wellbeing of a nation.
	
 It can easily promote instability in the country based on political differences thereby 
forcing mal administration.
	
 It undermines national unity and can easily lead to conflict
Learning Activities
Describing the history of Political Party Systems in Zambia
Learning Environment Set Up: 
This activity can be done using online research as well as the natural setting

Civic Education
Teaching Module
12
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
pictures of historical events, charts with Zambia’s political party history
Suggested Teaching Method: 
Case Study
 Activity Process
Case Study
Divide learners into groups and ask them to undertake a case study of Zambia’s 
political history under the following themes:
a.	 Multi-party system in the First Republic
b.	 One- Party participatory system in the Second Republic
c.	 Multi-party system in the Third Republic 
In this research:
 
z learners to identify the salient features of one-party and multi-party systems in 
Zambia
 
z Learners to analyse the impact of the two systems on individual freedoms and 
general welfare of citizens.
 
z Learners to present their findings to the class in a diagrammatical presentation.

Expected Standard	
Political party systems evaluated accordingly
Assessment
1.	 Create a timeline of Zambia’s political party systems, highlighting key events, 
milestones and major changes. 
Read the scenario and answer the questions which follow
At independence Zambia inherited a multiparty system. However, the constitution 
of Zambia changed in 1973 recommending the establishment of a one-party par­
ticipatory democracy. All opposition political parties were banned leaving only the 
ruling United National Independence party (UNIP). In 1991, the country reverted

Key Terms
Local Government: Governance based on localised community at district level.

Mayor: The Political head of a city or Municipal Council

to local government.

deliberate on national legislative matters.

Content Tips 
	
 Before independence, the British Colonial administration ruled through tradition­
al leaders or native authorities.
	
 Native authorities assisted the colonial government in collecting taxes, issuing 
licences and presiding over civil cases.
	
 After independence, Zambia enacted the Local Government Act Number 69 of 
1965 which created four types of councils; City Councils, Municipal Councils 
Township Councils and Rural Councils.
	
 In 1980, the Local government and Decentralisation Act was enacted which 
placed all council under the control of District Governors
	
 In 1991, the Local Government and Decentralisation Act of 1980 was amended 
creating the current three types of autonomous councils; City (for cities), Munic­
ipal (For Provincial Headquarters and other big towns) and District Councils (for 
small towns or districts).
	
 The 2019 Local Government Act outlines the three main types of Local Councils 
based on the size and status of the area they govern. The three are City councils, 
Municipal Councils and Town Councils.

Content Tips
Article 169 of the Constitution of Zambia provides that there is established a House of 
Chiefs which shall consist of five chiefs from each province, elected by the chiefs in a 
Province, as prescribed.
According to Article 169 Clause 5 of the Constitution, the functions of the House of 
Chiefs are to:
(a)	 consider and discuss a Bill relating to custom or tradition referred to it by the 
President, before the Bill is introduced into the National Assembly;
(b)	 initiate, discuss and make recommendations to the National Assembly 
regarding socio-economic development in the province;
(c)	 initiate, discuss and decide on matters relating to customary law and practice;
(d)	 initiate, discuss and make recommendations to a local authority regarding the 
welfare of communities in a local authority;
(e)	 make proposals on areas in customary law that require codification;
(f)	 advise the Government on traditional and customary matters; and
(g)	 perform other functions as prescribed.

Key Terms
Budgeting: tracking income and expenses to ensure spending is within limits and

Saving: setting aside a portion of income for emergencies, future needs, or retirement.

tax liabilities.

transaction in an economy.

consecutive quarters.

Content tips 
The concept money
	
z Money is anything of value used to buy goods and to pay for services. It is a 
medium of exchange that is agreed upon or accepted to be used by society. 
	
z In ancient times people used to exchange one item for another. That was referred 
to as barter system. For example, people would use salt to exchange with gold. 
	
z It was later realized that barter system alone could not make trade easy because 
some article that were used in trade were very heavy and sometimes it was difficult 
to harmonise the article with the value of something that someone wanted. 
	
z With the rise in civilization, bank notes and coins were introduced to easy 
transactions. 
	
z The value of money is the amount of goods and services it buys or pays for. This 
means that the price is the value of the commodities and services expressed in 
monetary form. 
	
z The general increase in the price of goods and services reduces the purchasing 
power of money leading to inflation.

The following are some of the main characteristics of money:
	
z Portability- meaning money should be easy to carry. 
	
z Durability- meaning that money should last for a long time without being damaged.
	
z Divisibility- Meaning that it must be easy to divide money into smaller units. 
	
z Uniformity- This means that unit of money should be uniform in terms of design.
	
z Acceptability- meaning that money must be widely agreed upon and accepted by 
people as a means of exchange. 
	
z   Scarcity- Money should not be easy to find.

The functions of money are:
 
 Medium of exchange: it enables people to buy one item which they can later sell to

Explaining the Law of demand and supply
Learning Environment Setup: 
This activity can be carried out from a classroom environment
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Toy goods (can be small objects like balls, books, etc.), price cards, paper, pen, or dig­
ital devices for surveys.
Suggested Teaching Method: 
Guest Speaker
Activity Process
	
z Invite a business person to come and present to the learners how the law of demand 
and supply influences their business.
	
z Leaners to ask questions based on the explanation of the guest
	
z Class to hold a plenary session to discuss the issues raised by the guest speaker 
on demand and supply.
Content Tips
	
z Demand is the ability and willingness of people to pay for goods and services.
	
z Supply is the availability of goods and services on the market. 
	
z The law of demand and supply explains the interaction between the two concepts.
	
z This law states that the price of the product will go down if the supply increases 
or the demand for the product is low. On the contrary, the price of the product will 
go up if the supply decreases or if the demand for the product is high. 
	
z Sometimes the demand can match the supply. This is called equilibrium point. 
Here, the price of the goods seems to be fair to both the customers and the sellers.
	
z High Demand + Low Supply= High Price 
	
z Low Demand + High Supply= Low Price
	
z Moderate Demand + Moderate Supply= Fair Price (equilibrium)
	
z High Demand + High Supply= High Price (exception to the law of demand and 
supply)

Content tips
Money Laundering 
	
z Money laundering means making money which has been acquired illegally look 
clean. This is done by passing illegally acquired money through a legitimate 
business or bank account in order to disguise its illegal origins. 
Money laundering activities
	
z Money laundering activities include; Fraud, concealment or deception, theft, 
counterfeiting, collusion, forgery, false pretence among others.
	
z People who practice money laundering are those involved in drug trafficking, 
bribery, smuggling, terrorism and sell of illegal arms among others.
Effects of Money Laundering
	
z Leads to an increase in corruption and crime.
	
z Undermines national revenue collection 
	
z Damages the integrity of the country 
	
z undermines democracy and the rule of law.
	
z Affects direct foreign investment

1.	 Business idea Evaluation Worksheet
	
z Organise learners in groups 
	
z Provide each group with worksheets to help them evaluate different business

 Market demand: who is your target customer? Is there a need for the product/

 Costs and Resources: what will you need to start this business? (e.g., materials,

2.	 SWOT Analysis for Business Ideas
	
z Provide learners with a template that divides a page into four sections: Strengths, 
Weaknesses, Opportunities and Threats (SWOT Analysis Template).
	
z In groups, learners will evaluate several business ideas using the SWOT analysis 
framework. For example:
 
 Strengths: what are the advantages of this business idea? (e.g., low start-up 
cost, unique product).
 
 Weaknesses: what are the drawback or challenges? (e.g., high competition, 
lack of initial investment).
 
 Opportunities: are there external factors that could benefit the business? (e.g., 
growing market demand, new technology)
 
 Threats: What external factors could harm the business? (e.g., economic 
downturn, changes in laws).
	
z Have each group present their SWOT analysis and discuss which business ideas 
appear to have the most potential based on their strengths and opportunities, and 
how to mitigate weaknesses and threats.

 How to Evaluate Business Opportunities: 
Certain steps have to be followed to help someone to evaluate business opportunities. 
The following are some of the step guides:
	
z Alignment with goals. Ensure the opportunity is in line with your objectives, 
values and mission.
	
z Market demand. Make a survey of the market to determine if there is a genuine 
demand for the product or service
	
z Competitive landscape. Analyse the competition so that you understand the 
market’s saturation and to see the potential to engage in something different
	
z Target audience. Identify the target audience and their needs and preferences.
	
z Market size and growth. Make an estimate of the market size, growth potential 
and trends.
	
z Barriers to entry. Assess the ease or difficulty of entering the market, including 
regulatory requirements, competition and compliance.
	
z Revenue potential. Estimate the potential revenue and profit margins.

Introduction
This subtopic looks at saving and its importance. It also describes different ways of saving. 
Saving can be described as an act of setting aside a portion of one’s income or resources for 
future use and not spent immediately. Saving money is important because it ensures financial 
security and a peace of mind. Through this topic learners will learn the different ways of saving 
money and why saving is important.
Specific Competences
Learners to practice different ways of Saving

charts

Guest Speaker

1.	 Guest Speaker
	
z Invite a guest speaker (business or financial expert) to come and talk to the learners 
on the significance of saving money
	
z Learners to ask questions for clarity 
	
z Plenary session: class to engage into a discussion based on the guest speaker’s 
talk.
2.	 Start with an open discussion where learners can share their thoughts on why people 
save money. Guide them to discuss different reasons, such as:
	
z Saving for the future
	
z Avoiding debt
	
z Financial independence and security
	
z Reaching personal goals
	
z After the discussion, summarize key points and explain how saving provides

Content tips
Saving provides individuals, households, businesses and the broader economy with a number of 
significant benefits.  Some of the reasons why saving is significant are that it provides: 
 
 Financial Security:  Saving provides a safety net for unforeseen expenses, emergencies 
and financial shock. This reduces financial stress and anxiety.
 
 Investment or Wealth Creation: By putting aside part of one’s income, individuals 
can accumulate and grow wealth over time for use to achieve long- term financial goals.
 
 Independence: Saving give individuals financial independence. That enables them to 
make choices about their lives, careers and future. 
 
 Reduced Debt: Saving can help individuals reduce their debt burden by providing a 
source of funds for debt repayment. 
 
 Increased Financial Flexibility: Saving provides with financial flexibility to take 
advantage of new opportunities such investing in education or starting anew business.
 
 Improved Financial literacy: It helps individuals develop good financial habits and 
improve their financial literacy. This can lead to better financial decision-making and a 
stable financial future.

 Money is a tool used to buy things, save for the future, and make investments.
 
 Income is money coming in like pocket money or gift while Expenditure is money 
going out on expenses.
 
 Money has several key characteristics that make it effective for us to use in an economy. 
These include: durability, portability, divisibility, uniformity, acceptability and stability.
 
 Money saves four primary functions in an economy: medium of exchange, unit of 
account, store of value and standard of deferred payment.
 
 The law of demand and supply is a fundamental principle in economics that explains 
how the prices and quantities of goods and services are determined in the market.
 
 Money laundering is the process of disguising illegally obtained money as legitimate 
income to hide its criminal origin.
 
 Business opportunities refers to favourable set of circumstances that allow an 
entrepreneur or company to create and grow a profitable venture.
 
 A budget is a plan for how to use money wisely. It helps one to understands how much 
money they have and where it goes.
 
 Saving means putting aside some money for future use rather than spending it all. It is 
important to save because it helps in emergencies and for things one wants in future.
 
 Smart spending:  prioritise spending on needs first, then one has to decide if they wish 
to spend on wants based on how much money is left
